episode_meta.title: Cheryl Phillips: How AI Is Uncovering Hidden Stories in Local Government

guests.name: Cheryl Phillips
guests.role: Founder and Co-Director of Big Local News

ai_tools: Autofolio (entity extraction tool for police records)

use_cases.description: Agenda Watch: scraping and aggregating local government meeting agendas and minutes from hundreds of agencies by targeting the platform companies they contract with, enabling keyword search and pattern detection across jurisdictions
use_cases.outcome: Beta site with a couple hundred local governments; plan to scale to 3,000 agencies within six months; enables reporters covering 20+ cities to track issues across jurisdictions

use_cases.description: Audio analysis of public meetings to detect newsworthiness by analyzing volume spikes (shouting) and silence in eight-second intervals of meeting audio
use_cases.outcome: Found that both shouting and silence predicted newsworthiness in public meetings

use_cases.description: Marrying Agenda Watch corpus with federal ICE contracts data to identify California local governments that had ICE contracts while publicly claiming not to share data with ICE, in a sanctuary state
use_cases.organization: Big Local News, Stanford University, Bay City News
use_cases.outcome: Discovered Santa Clara County (Silicon Valley) had the most ICE contracts despite not being a border county; found city/county officials proclaiming non-cooperation with ICE the day after signing new ICE contracts; published story with Bay City News

use_cases.description: DataTalk: natural language querying of FEC campaign finance data (10 key tables) with built-in transparency showing SQL-like query steps, plain English explanations, caveats, and a shareable dashboard
use_cases.organization: Stanford Oval Lab, Big Local News, Brown Institute
use_cases.outcome: Used during 2024 presidential elections; student teams published stories with Honolulu Civil Beat, Bay City News, and Atlanta Journal Constitution; AJC team analyzed campaign spending in swing counties on abortion issues married with ad spending data

use_cases.description: Autofolio: generative AI entity extraction from police misconduct and use of force records (PDFs, audio, video) to build a public-facing database of police use of force and misconduct across California
use_cases.organization: Big Local News, Berkeley Institute for Data Science, Berkeley Investigative Reporting Program, UC Irvine Law Clinic, Community Law Enforcement Accountability Network
use_cases.outcome: Successfully extracting entities (weapon type, officer names, incident details); beginning of public-facing database; earlier manual analysis found 31-34 instances of Bakersfield police breaking bones with batons; follow-up story on mental health incidents

use_cases.description: Scraping WARN Act layoff notices from 44 states and piping news detection alerts via Slack channels organized by geography for subscribing newsrooms
use_cases.outcome: Baltimore Banner published three to four stories in recent months about USAID contractor layoffs discovered through Slack alerts; data published daily as open CSV on Big Local News platform

use_cases.description: Audit Watch: scraping federal audit clearinghouse daily for problematic audits of entities receiving $750,000+ in federal funds, with plans to add generative AI summarization to detect newsworthy findings
use_cases.outcome: Currently delivers tip alerts; goal is agentic AI solution that can distinguish truly newsworthy audit flags from minor accounting issues

use_cases.description: Starling Lab collaboration: authenticating and preserving police body camera footage using blockchain, creating immutable records with full chain of custody and audit trails
use_cases.tool_used: Blockchain (Starling Lab), hashing, notarization, P2P storage network
use_cases.organization: Big Local News, Starling Lab (Stanford), the GRIO
use_cases.outcome: Created authenticated archive of body cam footage with clickable provenance trail for readers; co-published with the GRIO; established chain of custody model including original police press releases as metadata

use_cases.description: Reuters photo authentication using Starling Lab technology, including January 6th Capitol coverage with provenance tracking from camera to wire service
use_cases.outcome: Case study comparing authenticated photos from Obama inauguration and January 6th at same locations; demonstrated transparent edit history via clickable interface

use_cases.description: Tracking legislative actions responding to Trump administration executive orders using BillTrack 50 data across all state legislatures and Congress, with generative AI summarization and confidence scoring
use_cases.organization: Big Local News, University of Missouri
use_cases.outcome: In development; goal is a public tool on Big Local News where anyone can sort and filter legislative responses to executive orders; student reporters will write stories for local newsrooms

use_cases.description: Ping Pong used as a walled-garden RAG tool for teaching: uploading tip sheets and course PDFs so students can query course materials in natural language without hallucination risks
use_cases.outcome: Students can quickly find information from uploaded course materials; safe experimentation environment

use_cases.description: Drug overdose data analysis revealing a cohort of older Black men (aged 51-70) dying at disproportionately higher rates in industrial and port cities, with data shared to 11+ local newsrooms for local reporting
use_cases.organization: Big Local News, New York Times, Baltimore Banner
use_cases.outcome: Major story published with New York Times and Baltimore Banner; county-level data with pre-built charts released for any newsroom to use; approximately 11 additional local newsrooms received tailored data for local stories

key_claims.claim: A local reporter today might have to cover 20 cities, whereas 15 years ago it might have been one or two
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Local government agencies often contract with just a handful of platform companies for agendas and minutes, making scalable scraping possible by targeting the platforms rather than individual agencies
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Santa Clara County, in the heart of Silicon Valley, had the most ICE contracts in California despite not being a border county
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Silence in public meetings predicted newsworthiness, which was unexpected (in addition to shouting)
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: There are over 700 police agencies in the state of California
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Bakersfield police broke people's bones in 31 to 34 instances when they used batons
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: WARN Act layoff notices can be scraped for 44 out of 50 US states
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Any entity receiving $750,000 or more from the federal government must submit a fiscal audit every year to the federal audit clearinghouse
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: A going concern letter in an audit always indicates newsworthiness, meaning the entity is in danger of not being able to continue in business
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: About a dozen local stories came out of a single nursing home staffing data set after providing training and story recipes to rural newsrooms
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Video analysis AI tools were too limited a few years ago but are now becoming capable enough to extract meaning from large volumes of police body camera footage
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

key_claims.claim: Baltimore Banner published three to four stories in recent months about USAID contractor layoffs discovered through Big Local News Slack alerts
key_claims.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

challenges.description: Police misconduct records come in a variety of formats (PDFs, audio, video), are not organized well, and the volume is too much for manual analysis across 700+ agencies
Local police agencies are allowed to delete public records after a certain amount of time, requiring proactive collection and preservation before records disappear
Nuance and context in police PDFs make entity extraction difficult; generative AI still struggles with the complexity of these documents
AI-generated code solutions are not always correct, and journalists without coding literacy may not be able to identify errors, making it like running with scissors
Matching police report narratives to body camera video to verify accuracy remains an unsolved AI problem due to the complexity of comparing text descriptions with visual events
Students using AI to get answers without understanding how to verify correctness independently; risk of unquestioning AI consumption
Audit watch tip alerts require domain expertise to interpret; most audit flags have nuance that non-specialist reporters cannot assess without AI assistance
Campaign finance data involves complex tables, interrelationships, and rules that require data journalism expertise or specialized tools to navigate
Government information is disappearing from the web at an accelerating rate, compounded by link rot and deliberate removal of federal data
Deep fakes and AI-generated misinformation threaten public trust in authentic media, making provenance and authentication critical
Funding constraints limit the ability to scale federated systems for police accountability across multiple states

quotes.text: It's really centered about around lowering the cost of doing accountability journalism, especially for local newsrooms, which as we know are resource strapped.
quotes.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

quotes.text: We were able to identify moments in public meetings, like several years worth of public meetings where city county officials essentially proclaimed that they were not sharing data with ICE, even though they had just inked new contracts with ICE, like the day before.
quotes.speaker: Cheryl Phillips
quotes.topic: AI-enabled accountability finding through cross-referencing data

quotes.text: As the agent does its work, it tells you what it's doing. So you ask that question. And it says thinking and it tells you all the steps it's doing. I'm going to this database, I'm going to this table, this table, this table, this table... And then it explains what it did, both in plain English, and it gives you the code.
quotes.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

quotes.text: I think it's a little bit like running with scissors, so you have to be careful. But I do think there's a lot of potential.
quotes.speaker: Cheryl Phillips
quotes.topic: AI for learning to code in journalism

quotes.text: We want to avoid people going and saying, Oh, this must be right because I asked the AI and the AI said. And so that's where we try to really introduce those critical thinking skills and a little code literacy goes a long way.
quotes.speaker: Cheryl Phillips
quotes.topic: Teaching critical evaluation of AI outputs

quotes.text: It increases the impact for the big data teams if they share it back out, and it makes it possible for the small data teams to be able to cover stories they would otherwise completely miss.
quotes.speaker: Cheryl Phillips
quotes.topic: DART Matrix virtuous cycle of data sharing

recommendations.advice: Use Copilot (GitHub) if doing any kind of coding; it is a great way to get started with AI-assisted development
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: Explore Hugging Face as an open source platform with models, tools, and tutorials to get started with AI without depending on proprietary services like ChatGPT
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: Use the DART Matrix framework (Data, Algorithms, Recipes, Training) to assess newsroom data capacity and match appropriate tools and support to capability level
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: Teach coding fundamentals before introducing AI tools; discourage AI use until about three quarters through the curriculum so students build critical evaluation skills first
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: When students submit perfect code, talk to them about it; their code should not be perfect in intro classes, they should be struggling and learning
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: Use Ping Pong (Harvard tool) to create walled-garden AI environments for classrooms where students can query uploaded course materials without hallucination risks
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: For newsrooms with zero data experts, provide story recipes and hands-on training rather than raw data feeds; for teams with three or more data experts, provide data streams and APIs they can build on
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: Build open source agentic tools rather than depending on proprietary technology companies; the fact that tools like Stanford Oval's agent are open source is a game changer for journalism
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips

recommendations.advice: Preserve digital records proactively, especially government data that may be deleted or removed; use blockchain-based authentication for critical evidentiary material
recommendations.speaker: Cheryl Phillips
